questions.text: How does this Surah define the spiritual danger of ‘takathur’?
questions.answer: It turns life into rivalry for more, making people forget Allah, death, and accountability.

questions.text: True/False: The Surah teaches that rivaling others in wealth is harmless if you stay polite.
questions.answer: False. It condemns rivalry that distracts from the Hereafter.

questions.text: What does 'until you visit the graves' teach about life’s end?
questions.answer: Death interrupts all competitions; when it arrives, deeds stop and truth becomes clear.

questions.text: What is one modern form of 'takathur' besides money?
questions.answer: Competing in followers, status, luxury, achievements, or social reputation.

questions.text: Why does the Surah repeat the warning 'you will surely know'?
questions.answer: To intensify urgency and show that denial will turn into certainty when reality appears.

questions.text: What does 'knowledge of certainty' teach about faith?
questions.answer: Faith can give intellectual certainty now through revelation, before seeing the Hereafter.

questions.text: What does 'eye of certainty' teach about the Hereafter?
questions.answer: Seeing the reality later is certainty by sight, but it comes too late to change deeds.

questions.text: True/False: The Surah suggests seeing punishment is needed before taking religion seriously.
questions.answer: False. It urges taking it seriously now through knowledge of certainty.

questions.text: What does being questioned about blessings (na'im) mean practically?
questions.answer: You will be asked how you used health, time, wealth, and opportunities—gratitude includes responsible use.

questions.text: What is one daily practice to prepare for being questioned about blessings?
questions.answer: Use one blessing each day in obedience (prayer on time, charity, helping someone, avoiding sin).

questions.text: How does the Surah correct the idea that ‘more’ always equals ‘better’?
questions.answer: It shows ‘more’ can be a distraction and a heavier account, not a sign of success.

questions.text: True/False: The Surah teaches Islam forbids earning wealth.
questions.answer: False. It condemns rivalry and distraction that causes neglect of Allah.

questions.text: How can someone earn wealth without falling into takathur?
questions.answer: Keep prayer and ethics first, give charity, avoid showing off, and treat wealth as a tool for the Hereafter.

questions.text: How does visiting graves benefit the heart in line with this Surah?
questions.answer: It breaks illusions of endless life and resets priorities toward repentance and good deeds.

questions.text: What is one misconception corrected by this Surah about success?
questions.answer: Success is not having more than others; success is meeting Allah with gratitude and obedience.

questions.text: True/False: The Surah teaches that people will only be asked about haram blessings, not halal ones.
questions.answer: False. It teaches people will be questioned about blessings in general.

questions.text: What does 'At-Takathur' mean?
questions.answer: Competition in increase (of worldly things) or mutual rivalry in accumulation.

questions.text: What does this Surah say distracts people?
questions.answer: The mutual rivalry in seeking more wealth, status, and children.

questions.text: Until when does this distraction continue for many?
questions.answer: Until they visit the graves (death).

questions.text: What is the repeated warning in this Surah?
questions.answer: Nay, you are going to know; then nay, you are going to know (the reality of your choices).

questions.text: What is 'Ilm al-Yaqin'?
questions.answer: The Knowledge of Certainty (intellectual certainty through revelation).

questions.text: What does this Surah warn everyone will surely see?
questions.answer: Hellfire (Al-Jahim).

questions.text: What is 'Ayn al-Yaqin'?
questions.answer: The Eye of Certainty (seeing the reality with one's own eyes).

questions.text: What will happen on that Day regarding 'favors' (An-Na'im)?
questions.answer: Every person will be questioned about the blessings and favors they enjoyed in the world.

questions.text: What are some examples of 'Na'im' (favors) we will be asked about?
questions.answer: Health, time, wealth, food, water, and our senses.

questions.text: Does 'visiting the graves' mean just visiting a cemetery?
questions.answer: No, it is a euphemism for death and entering the grave as an inhabitant.

questions.text: What is the core disease of the heart addressed in this Surah?
questions.answer: Materialism and being consumed by worldly competition at the expense of the Hereafter.

questions.text: Why is 'Nay' (Kalla) used in this Surah?
questions.answer: To strongly rebuke and stop people from their current path of distraction.

questions.text: Is seeking wealth forbidden in Islam based on this Surah?
questions.answer: No, it is the *rivalry* and *distraction* that leads to neglecting Allah that is condemned.

questions.text: What is 'Haqq al-Yaqin' (mentioned in other Surahs) compared to these?
questions.answer: It is the 'Absolute Truth' of experiencing the reality, such as entering Paradise or Hell.

questions.text: What should be the believer's response to the favors of Allah?
questions.answer: Gratitude (Shukr) and using them in ways that please Him.

questions.text: Does this Surah imply that death ends the distraction?
questions.answer: Yes, but by then it is too late to change one's deeds.

questions.text: What is the tone of the warning in the middle of the Surah?
questions.answer: Severe and repetitive to ensure the reader wakes up from heedlessness.

questions.text: How many times will people be questioned about favors?
questions.answer: It will be an exhaustive questioning on the Day of Judgment.

questions.text: What is the final word of the Surah?
questions.answer: An-Na'im (The Favors/Blessings).
